title: Advanced concessive and contrast structures
lesson: Formal English offers a rich set of concessive structures that go well beyond 'although'. 'Much as I admire her' fronts the verb of feeling for emphasis ('Much as + subject + verb'). 'However much/hard + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of effort changes the outcome. 'For all + noun phrase' means 'in spite of' ('For all his talent, he failed'). 'Granted (that) + clause' concedes a point before countering it. 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, typically an adjective or adverbial, not a full finite clause. 'Notwithstanding' functions as a preposition ('notwithstanding the objections') or, more formally, follows its noun. These patterns differ chiefly in what must follow them, which is what tells the right connector apart from its near-synonyms.

items.text: {1} I admire her courage, I simply cannot support such a reckless decision.

blanks.hint: fronts a verb of feeling: ___ + subject + verb

items.explanation: 'Much as I admire her courage' is the fixed concessive pattern 'Much as + subject + verb' meaning 'although I greatly admire'. 'As much' inverts the words and is ungrammatical here; 'Despite' must be followed by a noun or -ing, not a finite clause; 'However' would need an adjective/adverb ('However much').

items.text: {1} hard she tried, she could never quite master the pronunciation.

blanks.hint: ___ + adverb + subject + verb = no degree of effort helped

items.explanation: 'However + adverb (hard) + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of trying changed the result. 'Much as' takes a verb directly, not an adverb; 'Whatever' takes a noun/clause, not the adverb 'hard'; 'Albeit' cannot introduce a finite clause like 'she tried'.

items.text: {1} his undeniable talent, he never achieved the recognition he deserved.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase = in spite of

items.explanation: 'For all + noun phrase' means 'in spite of' / 'despite': 'For all his talent' = 'despite his talent'. 'Much as' and 'However' require a following clause/adjective, not a bare noun phrase; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase that qualifies something already stated, not a fronted 'in spite of' phrase.

items.text: {1} that the proposal has merit, we still lack the funds to implement it this year.

blanks.hint: concede a point before countering: ___ (that) + clause

items.explanation: 'Granted (that) + clause' concedes a point you are about to argue against: 'Granted that the proposal has merit, we still...'. 'Albeit' cannot take a 'that'-clause; 'Despite' takes a noun phrase or -ing, never a finite 'that'-clause; 'Much as' is followed directly by subject + verb ('Much as the proposal has merit'), not by 'that'.

items.text: The committee approved the revised budget, {1} with considerable reluctance.

blanks.hint: introduces a concessive phrase (no finite verb), here 'with reluctance'

items.explanation: 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase qualifying what precedes it: 'approved..., albeit with considerable reluctance'. The other options all require a following clause or different structure ('much as'/'granted' need a subject and verb; 'however' would need an adjective/adverb), so none fits a bare prepositional phrase.

items.text: {1} the strenuous objections of the board, the merger went ahead as planned.

blanks.hint: a formal preposition: ___ + noun phrase = in spite of

items.explanation: 'Notwithstanding' is a formal preposition meaning 'in spite of', so it is followed directly by the noun phrase 'the strenuous objections'. 'Much as' requires a subject + verb ('Much as the board objected'); 'However' needs a following adjective/adverb; 'Even though' introduces a finite clause, not a bare noun phrase.

items.text: Hard {1} he tried, he never managed to win her approval.

blanks.hint: fronted adjective/adverb + ___ + subject + verb

items.explanation: The fronted concessive pattern 'Adjective/Adverb + though + subject + verb' ('Hard though he tried') is an emphatic equivalent of 'although he tried hard'. 'Despite' takes a noun/-ing, not a clause; 'however' would itself stand at the front ('However hard he tried'), not after the fronted word; 'much' does not link a fronted adverb to a clause in this slot.

items.text: Exhausted {1} she was, she insisted on finishing the marathon.

blanks.hint: fronted adjective + ___ + subject + be = concessive 'as ... as'

items.explanation: 'Adjective + as + subject + be' ('Exhausted as she was') is a concessive inversion meaning 'although she was exhausted', common in formal and literary English. 'Though as' doubles the connector and is ungrammatical; 'however' would precede the adjective ('However exhausted she was'); 'despite' cannot govern the finite clause 'she was'.

items.text: The plan is undeniably bold. {1}, I remain convinced it can be made to work.

blanks.hint: a conjunct linking back to the previous sentence: 'in spite of that'

items.explanation: 'Even so' is a sentence conjunct meaning 'in spite of that', linking back to the preceding sentence and standing alone before a comma. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate clause within the same sentence; 'Much as' needs a following subject + verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a full main clause.

items.text: {1} the figures may look discouraging, they conceal a genuine improvement in efficiency.

blanks.hint: subordinator used concessively: ___ + finite clause = although

items.explanation: 'While' (or 'Whilst') used concessively introduces a finite clause and means 'although': 'While the figures may look discouraging'. 'For all' and 'Notwithstanding' are followed by a noun phrase, not a finite clause; 'Albeit' cannot head a full finite clause with its own subject and modal ('the figures may look').

items.text: {1} much the government spends on the scheme, the underlying problem will not disappear.

blanks.hint: ___ + much + subject + verb = no amount of it helps

items.explanation: 'However + much + subject + verb' concedes that no quantity of something (here, spending) changes the outcome: 'However much the government spends' = 'no matter how much it spends'. 'For all' and 'Notwithstanding' must be followed by a noun phrase, not by 'much + clause'; 'Much as' takes a verb of feeling directly ('Much as I dislike it'), not the quantifier 'much' plus another clause.

items.text: The treatment proved effective, {1} slower to take hold than the doctors had hoped.

blanks.hint: introduces a concessive phrase (here an adjective), not a finite clause

items.explanation: 'Albeit' qualifies a preceding statement with a concessive adjective or adverbial phrase: 'effective, albeit slower to take hold'. 'Even so' is a free-standing conjunct that links whole sentences and would need its own main clause; 'Much as' and 'While' both require a following finite clause with a subject and verb, not the bare adjective phrase 'slower to take hold'.

items.text: {1} as he might, he could not persuade the committee to reconsider.

blanks.hint: fixed pattern: bare verb + as + subject + verb = 'although he tried'

items.explanation: 'Try as + subject + verb' is a fixed concessive idiom ('Try as he might/did') meaning 'however hard he tried'. It fronts the bare verb 'Try' before 'as'. 'However' would itself front the clause and take an adverb ('However hard he tried'); 'Despite' governs a noun or -ing, not a finite clause; 'Much' does not begin this fronted 'as'-pattern, which requires the verb in the gap.

items.text: {1} much we cut costs, the project will still run well over budget.

blanks.hint: ___ + 'much' + subject + verb = no degree of it changes the outcome

items.explanation: 'However much + subject + verb' concedes that no amount of the action alters the result: 'However much we cut costs' = 'No matter how much we cut costs'. 'For all' is a preposition taking a noun phrase ('For all our cost-cutting'), not 'much' + a clause; 'Albeit' cannot introduce a finite clause with its own subject and verb; 'Much as' is followed directly by the verb ('Much as we cut costs'), so adding 'much' after it is ungrammatical.

items.text: The results were encouraging, {1} preliminary and based on a small sample.

blanks.hint: introduces a bare concessive adjective phrase qualifying what precedes

items.explanation: 'Albeit' introduces a reduced concessive phrase — here the bare adjectives 'preliminary and based on a small sample' — qualifying the preceding clause; it is equivalent to 'although they were'. 'Even so' is a free-standing conjunct that begins a new main clause, not a phrase tagged on after a comma; 'much as' requires a following subject and verb ('much as they were preliminary'); 'despite' is a preposition that must govern a noun or -ing form ('despite their preliminary nature'), not a bare predicative adjective.

items.text: {1} as he might, he could not budge the rusted bolt a single millimetre.

blanks.hint: fronted bare verb + 'as' + subject + 'might' = an emphatic 'no matter how hard he tried'

items.explanation: The fixed idiom 'Try as he might' fronts the bare verb before 'as + subject + might', meaning 'however hard he tried'. The slot requires a verb, so only 'Try' fits the pattern '[verb] as he might'. 'However' would be followed by an adjective/adverb, not stand before 'as he might'; 'Much as' takes a verb of feeling directly ('Much as he tried') without the modal 'might' construction; 'Albeit' cannot govern a finite clause at all.

items.text: The forecast points to a sharp downturn. {1}, the board has approved an ambitious expansion.

blanks.hint: a fixed formal conjunct: 'even if that is true / in spite of that'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed, formal sentence conjunct meaning 'even if that is so / in spite of that'; it stands alone before a comma and concedes the previous sentence while introducing a contrasting one. 'Much as it is' is not an idiomatic concessive opener and leaves the verb dangling; 'However it be' is archaic and ungrammatical in modern usage; 'Granted that' must be followed by a clause it concedes ('Granted that the forecast is grim'), not used as a free-standing reference back to the previous sentence.

items.text: {1} the obstacles, the expedition was determined to reach the pole.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase = 'no matter what'; concedes any/every member of a set

items.explanation: 'Whatever + noun phrase' is a concessive determiner meaning 'no matter what': 'Whatever the obstacles' = 'No matter what the obstacles were'. It licenses a bare noun phrase with the verb 'be' understood. 'Much as' and 'While' require a finite clause with subject and verb ('Much as obstacles arose'); 'However' would need a following adjective/adverb ('However many obstacles'), not the bare noun 'the obstacles'.

items.text: {1} as he might, he could not force the rusted lock to turn.

blanks.hint: fixed pattern: bare verb + as + subject + 'might/may'

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is the fixed concessive inversion 'verb + as + subject + might/may', meaning 'however hard he tried'. The slot needs the bare verb 'Try'. 'However' would have to front the whole clause and take an adverb ('However hard he tried'); 'Despite' governs a noun or -ing, not 'as he might'; 'Albeit' introduces a phrase, not a finite 'as'-clause with a modal.

items.text: Your reservations are understandable. {1}, the board has decided that the project must proceed.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct meaning 'even so / nevertheless'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'even so / that may be true, but', standing alone before a comma and linking back to the previous sentence. 'Much as it may' is incomplete (it needs a following verb of feeling, e.g. 'Much as it may pain me'); 'However that is' is not an idiomatic connector; 'Albeit so' is not standard English, as 'albeit' introduces a phrase, not a free-standing sentence opener.

items.text: {1} though I respect his experience, I cannot accept his conclusion on this point.

blanks.hint: intensifier + 'though' + subject + verb of feeling

items.explanation: 'Much though + subject + verb' is an emphatic concessive equivalent of 'Much as I respect his experience' / 'although I greatly respect'. The slot needs the intensifier 'Much' before 'though'. 'However' would replace 'though' and take an adjective/adverb, not precede it; 'Despite' takes a noun or -ing, not 'though + clause'; 'As' would produce 'As though', which means 'as if', not concession.

items.text: {1} you decide, you can be sure the team will stand behind you completely.

blanks.hint: ___ + subject + verb (object understood) = 'no matter what'

items.explanation: 'Whatever + subject + verb' is the concessive 'no matter what (thing)': 'Whatever you decide' = 'no matter what you decide'. The verb 'decide' here lacks an object, so the pronoun 'Whatever' supplies it and functions as its object. 'Despite' is a preposition taking a noun or -ing, so it cannot head the finite clause 'you decide'; 'Much as' takes a verb of feeling directly and adds no 'no matter what' sense; 'Albeit' cannot head a finite clause with its own subject. (Note 'However' is avoided as a distractor here: 'However you decide' = 'in whatever manner you decide' would also be acceptable, so it is not clearly wrong.)

items.text: {1} the fact that costs had tripled, the council refused to cancel the festival.

blanks.hint: preposition phrase + 'the fact that' + clause = despite

items.explanation: 'In spite of' is a prepositional phrase, so to govern a finite clause it must take the noun 'the fact' followed by a 'that'-clause: 'In spite of the fact that costs had tripled'. 'Even though' introduces a finite clause directly and cannot stand before 'the fact that' here ('Even though the fact that...' is ungrammatical); 'Much as' takes subject + verb, not 'the fact that'; 'Albeit' introduces a phrase, not 'the fact that' + clause.

items.text: {1} as he might, he could not prise the rusted lid from the jar.

blanks.hint: bare verb + 'as he might' = no matter how hard he tried

items.explanation: The fixed emphatic concessive 'Try as he might' uses a bare verb + 'as + subject + might/may' to mean 'however hard he tried'. 'Trying as he might' is ungrammatical because the pattern demands the bare infinitive form, not the -ing participle; 'However' would need a following adverb ('However hard he tried') and no 'as'; 'Much' fits the different frame 'Much as he tried' (verb of feeling/effort) but not before the 'as he might' already supplied here.

items.text: The route is longer and the tolls are higher. {1}, I would still rather drive than face the airport.

blanks.hint: fixed conjunct conceding the previous point: 'even if all that is true'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'even if that is so', standing alone before a comma and conceding the whole preceding statement. 'Such as it is' qualifies a noun ('the food, such as it is'), not a previous sentence; 'As it were' softens a figurative expression ('a digital nomad, as it were') and is not concessive; 'Come what may' means 'whatever happens' and looks to the future rather than conceding an established fact.

items.text: {1} how persuasively they argue, the committee is unlikely to reverse its decision.

blanks.hint: ___ + how + adverb + subject + verb = to whatever degree

items.explanation: 'No matter + how + adverb + subject + verb' ('No matter how persuasively they argue') concedes that the degree of an action changes nothing. 'However' carries the same meaning but takes the adverb directly without 'how' ('However persuasively they argue'), so it clashes with the 'how' already in the sentence; 'Whatever' precedes a noun or clause, not 'how'; 'Much as' is followed straight by subject + verb, not by 'how'.

items.text: {1} as it may seem, the quietest applicant turned out to be the most demanding negotiator.

blanks.hint: bare adjective + 'as it may seem' = though it seems strange

items.explanation: 'Strange as it may seem' is a concessive inversion of the form 'adjective + as + subject + may + seem', meaning 'although it may seem strange'. The slot requires the bare adjective 'Strange' because 'seem' is a copular verb that takes an adjective complement, so the adverb 'Strangely' is wrong; 'However' would need to precede the adjective and drop 'as' ('However strange it may seem'); 'Albeit' introduces a phrase, not this inverted clause with its own subject and modal.

items.text: The northern branch posted record profits this quarter, {1} the southern branch barely broke even.

blanks.hint: subordinator of direct contrast between two clauses: 'while by contrast'

items.explanation: 'Whereas' is a subordinator of direct contrast, introducing a finite clause that is set against the main clause: '..., whereas the southern branch barely broke even'. 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a full clause with its own subject and verb; 'Notwithstanding' is a preposition taking a noun phrase ('notwithstanding the figures'); 'Much as' is followed by a verb of feeling and means 'although', not the balanced 'by contrast' relationship needed here.

items.text: {1} he might, he could never persuade the committee to reconsider its verdict.

blanks.hint: fronts a bare verb of effort: ___ + subject + modal (might/would)

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is the fixed concessive pattern 'Try as + subject + might/would', fronting the bare verb 'try' for emphasis and meaning 'however hard he tried'. 'However' requires a following adjective or adverb ('However hard he tried'), not the bare verb 'try'; 'Much as' is followed by a verb of feeling or attitude expressing degree ('Much as he admired'), not by the modal pattern 'try as ... might'; 'Granted' concedes a point and must be followed by a noun or a (that-)clause ('Granted his effort'), not by 'as he might'.

items.text: The risks are considerable, and the timing is poor. {1}, the board has resolved to proceed.

blanks.hint: a fixed concessive conjunct linking back: 'that may be so, but...'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed subjunctive concessive conjunct meaning 'even though that is so', standing alone before a comma and a full main clause that follows. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate finite clause within the sentence, not stand free before a main clause; 'Much as' requires a following subject + verb of feeling ('Much as the board regrets it'); 'For all' must be followed by a noun phrase ('For all the risks'), not used as a free-standing connector.

items.text: {1} carefully you phrase the request, he is bound to take offence at it.

blanks.hint: ___ + adverb + subject + verb = to whatever degree, it makes no difference

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adverb (carefully) + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of care will change the outcome, exactly parallel to 'However carefully'. 'Whatever' takes a noun or noun phrase ('Whatever words you use'), not the adverb 'carefully'; 'Even if' introduces a conditional finite clause and cannot govern a fronted adverb of degree; 'Despite' is a preposition requiring a noun or -ing form, never an adverb followed by subject + verb.

items.text: {1} as he might, he could not budge the rusted bolt.

blanks.hint: bare infinitive + 'as' + subject + 'might' = however hard he tried

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is a fixed concessive pattern 'bare verb + as + subject + might', meaning 'however hard he tried', and is followed by a negative main clause. 'However' would itself head the clause ('However hard he tried') rather than sit before 'as'; 'Much' begins 'Much as he tried' but cannot combine with the modal 'might' in this slot; 'Despite' is a preposition taking a noun or '-ing', not this inverted clause.

items.text: The scheme carries obvious risks. {1}, the potential rewards are too great to ignore.

blanks.hint: a free-standing formal conjunct = 'in spite of that', before a comma and a full clause

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed formal conjunct meaning 'in spite of that', which stands alone before a comma and links back to the previous sentence while introducing a full main clause. 'Much as' must be followed directly by subject + verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing clause; 'No matter' is incomplete without 'how/what' and a following clause ('No matter how risky').

items.text: {1} how carefully you phrase it, the message will offend someone.

blanks.hint: ___ + 'how' + adverb + subject + verb = it makes no difference

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adverb + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of care changes the outcome and requires the following 'how'. 'However' carries the same meaning but is not itself followed by a separate 'how' ('However carefully you phrase it'), so it cannot precede 'how' here; 'Despite' and 'For all' take a noun phrase, not the finite clause 'how carefully you phrase it'.

items.text: {1} reservations he may privately harbour, in public he supports the policy without question.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun + subject + 'may' + verb = no matter what

items.explanation: 'Whatever + noun + subject + may + verb' is a concessive pattern meaning 'no matter what', here governing the noun 'reservations' ('Whatever reservations he may harbour'). 'However' would take an adjective/adverb, not the noun 'reservations'; 'Albeit' cannot head a finite clause with its own subject and modal; 'Much as' is followed directly by a verb of feeling, not by a noun object.

items.text: Strange {1} it may seem, the smallest department returned the largest profit that year.

blanks.hint: fronted adjective + ___ + subject + verb = concessive 'as'

items.explanation: 'Adjective + as + subject + verb' ('Strange as it may seem') is a concessive inversion meaning 'although it may seem strange', a fixed pattern in formal and literary English. 'Though as' doubles the connector and is ungrammatical; 'however' would precede the adjective ('However strange it may seem'); 'despite' cannot govern the finite clause 'it may seem'.

items.text: The costs are admittedly high. {1}, the long-term benefits more than justify the investment.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct conceding the previous point: 'even if that is true'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'even if that is true', standing alone before a comma and conceding the preceding sentence before countering it. 'Much as it may' and 'However it may' are incomplete fragments that demand a following verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a full main clause.

items.text: {1} as he might, he could not prise the lid off the jar.

blanks.hint: fixed pattern: bare verb + 'as he might' = 'however hard he tried'

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is a fixed concessive idiom ('Bare verb + as + subject + might') meaning 'however hard he tried'. The verb must be the bare infinitive form 'Try'; the participles 'Trying'/'Tried' break the fixed pattern, and 'However' would require an adverb and a different structure ('However hard he tried').

items.text: I see no reason to change course now. {1}, I am willing to review the decision in six months.

blanks.hint: a conjunct qualifying one's own previous statement: 'having said that'

items.explanation: 'That said' is a fixed conjunct ('having said that') used to introduce a qualification or partial concession to one's own preceding statement, standing alone before a comma. 'Said that' reverses the fixed order and is ungrammatical here; 'Much as' needs a following subject + verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a main clause.

items.text: {1} though I respect his judgement, I cannot agree on this occasion.

blanks.hint: fronted intensifier + 'though' + subject + verb

items.explanation: 'Much though I respect his judgement' uses the pattern 'Much + though + subject + verb', an emphatic equivalent of 'although I greatly respect'. 'However' would itself head the clause ('However much I respect'), not sit before 'though'; 'Despite' takes a noun/-ing, not a finite clause; 'As' would require the intensifier to precede it ('Much as I respect'), not follow with 'though'.

items.text: The evidence against him was overwhelming. {1}, the jury chose to acquit.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct meaning 'in spite of that / nevertheless'

items.explanation: 'For all that' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'in spite of that / nevertheless', linking back to the preceding sentence and standing alone before a comma. It differs from 'For all + noun phrase' ('For all his talent'): 'For all' alone cannot stand before a comma without a following noun; 'Much as' demands a following subject + verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a full main clause.

items.text: Try {1} he might, he could not persuade the panel to reconsider its verdict.

blanks.hint: fixed emphatic pattern: 'Try ___ + subject + might/may'

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is a fixed concessive idiom ('verb + as + subject + might/may') meaning 'however hard he tried'. 'Though' does not occur in this frozen pattern after the bare verb 'Try'; 'however' would have to stand at the front of the clause ('However hard he tried'); 'much' does not link the fronted verb to the modal here.

items.text: The forecast is gloomy. {1}, the board has decided to press ahead with the expansion.

blanks.hint: a fixed subjunctive conjunct meaning 'even if that is true'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed formulaic concession (a fossilised subjunctive) meaning 'even if that is so', standing alone before a comma and a full main clause. 'However much' and 'Much as' require a following clause within the same sentence; 'Granted that' must be followed by the conceded clause itself, not used as a free-standing link back to the previous sentence.

items.text: {1} I am to criticise a colleague, I cannot let this error pass unremarked.

blanks.hint: fronted adjective + ___ + subject + be = 'although I am reluctant'

items.explanation: The pattern 'Adjective + as + subject + be' ('Loath as I am') fronts the adjective for an emphatic concession meaning 'although I am reluctant'. 'Loath that' is ungrammatical, as 'that' cannot link a fronted predicative adjective to its clause; 'However loath' would require no second connector and a different word order ('However loath I am'); 'Much as' takes a verb of feeling, not the predicative adjective 'loath'.

items.text: {1} how eloquently he argues, the facts are simply not on his side.

blanks.hint: ___ + how/what/who + clause = it makes no difference that

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adverb + clause' concedes that the degree of eloquence is irrelevant to the outcome: 'No matter how eloquently he argues'. 'For all' and 'Notwithstanding' take a noun phrase, not 'how + clause'; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a 'how'-clause with its own finite verb.

items.text: {1} though I value your loyalty, I must ask you to report the matter honestly.

blanks.hint: ___ + though + subject + verb (of feeling) = 'although I greatly...'

items.explanation: 'Much though + subject + verb' ('Much though I value your loyalty') is a variant of 'Much as', fronting the degree word before 'though' to mean 'although I greatly value'. 'As though' would mean 'as if', changing the sense entirely; 'However' needs an adjective/adverb directly after it, not 'though'; 'For all' must govern a noun phrase, not a finite clause introduced by 'though'.

items.text: {1} he might, he could not persuade them to reconsider the verdict.

blanks.hint: fronts a bare verb: ___ + subject + might/may

items.explanation: 'Try as + subject + might/may' is a fixed concessive pattern in which a bare verb is fronted and followed by 'as', the subject, and the modal 'might'/'may' ('Try as he might' = 'However hard he tried'). 'Try though' is impossible because 'though' does not take a fronted bare verb plus a modal in this slot; 'As try' simply reverses the words and is ungrammatical; 'Much as' must be followed directly by a finite verb of feeling ('Much as he tries'), not by a fronted infinitive plus 'might'.

items.text: The road ahead will be difficult. {1}, the government intends to press ahead with the reforms.

blanks.hint: fixed subjunctive conjunct = 'even granting all that'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive idiom using the subjunctive 'be'; it stands alone as a conjunct meaning 'even granting that' and links back to the preceding sentence. The distractors all scramble the fixed word order: 'However that may be it' adds a stray 'it'; 'As that may be' drops the obligatory subjunctive 'be' opening; and 'Be as it may that' mislinearises the components, since the idiom is frozen in exactly the order 'Be that as it may'.

items.text: {1} how forcefully they argue their case, the outcome has already been decided.

blanks.hint: ___ + how/what/who + clause = 'it makes no difference'

items.explanation: 'No matter + how/what/who + clause' concedes that the circumstance is irrelevant to the result, and here 'No matter how forcefully they argue' fits because 'how' supplies the adverb. 'However' cannot precede 'how' ('However how' is ungrammatical, as 'however' would itself replace 'how forcefully'); 'Whatever' takes a noun or noun-clause, not the adverbial 'how forcefully'; 'Despite' is a preposition requiring a noun phrase or -ing, not the finite clause 'how forcefully they argue'.

items.text: {1} reservations the panel may harbour, they are obliged to publish their findings in full.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun + subject + may + verb = 'any ... that'

items.explanation: 'Whatever' here functions as a concessive determiner before the noun 'reservations' ('Whatever reservations the panel may harbour' = 'any reservations that they may have, it makes no difference'). 'However' modifies an adjective or adverb, not a bare noun like 'reservations'; 'No matter' would require a following 'what' ('No matter what reservations'), so it cannot stand directly before the noun; 'Much as' must be followed by a subject and a finite verb, not by a noun phrase.

items.text: {1} as it may seem, the quietest applicant turned out to be the most persuasive of all.

blanks.hint: bare adjective + as + it + may seem = concessive comment

items.explanation: The frozen concessive comment 'Strange as it may seem' fronts the bare adjective 'Strange' before 'as it may seem', meaning 'although it may seem strange'. The adverb 'Strangely' cannot fill an adjective slot governed by the linking 'seem'; 'Though strange' doubles the concessive marker, since 'as' already carries the concessive force; 'However strange' belongs to a different pattern ('However strange it may seem') that has no room for the following 'as'.

items.text: Try {1} he might, he could not push the boulder even an inch.

blanks.hint: fixed pattern: Try ___ + subject + might/may = however hard one tries

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is a fixed concessive idiom built on the inverted pattern 'verb + as + subject + may/might', meaning 'however hard he tried'. The slot requires 'as': 'though' does not occur in this fronted-verb formula ('Try though he might' is not idiomatic standard English); 'however' would itself begin the clause and take an adverb ('However hard he tried'); 'much' cannot link the fronted bare verb 'Try' to the subject. The pattern is noted in the Cambridge Grammar of English as a literary/emphatic concessive (compare 'Search as they would').

items.text: The scheme is expensive and the timing is poor. {1}, the council voted to proceed.

blanks.hint: a fixed clause-conjunct meaning 'even if that is true'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive conjunct (a subjunctive formula) meaning 'even if that is so / in spite of that', standing alone before a comma and linking back to the previous sentence. The distractors are all non-existent or ill-formed: 'Much as that' lacks the required following subject + verb; 'However that' is not a standard connector and needs an adjective/adverb; 'Albeit so' is not idiomatic and 'albeit' cannot stand as a free sentence-opener before a full main clause. The formula is listed in Swan, Practical English Usage, among fixed subjunctive expressions.

items.text: {1} I respect the tradition, I believe the rule has outlived its usefulness.

blanks.hint: '___ + subject + verb', an emphatic variant of 'much as'

items.explanation: 'Much though I respect the tradition' is the emphatic concessive pattern 'Much though + subject + verb', a near-synonym of the more familiar 'Much as I respect…', both meaning 'although I greatly respect'. The fronted 'much' carries the degree and 'though' is the concessive linker. 'As much' simply inverts the words of 'much as' and is ungrammatical in this fronted position; 'For all' requires a bare noun phrase ('For all my respect for the tradition'), not the finite clause 'I respect the tradition'; 'Albeit' cannot head a full finite clause with its own subject and verb. The 'Adverb + though + subject + verb' inversion is described in the Cambridge Grammar of English.

items.text: The new policy is controversial; many staff dislike it. {1}, it has measurably cut waiting times.

blanks.hint: a conjunct meaning 'in spite of that', standing before a comma

items.explanation: 'For all that' is a sentence conjunct meaning 'in spite of all that / nevertheless', referring back to the previous statement and standing alone before a comma. Plain 'For all' is a preposition needing a following noun phrase ('For all its faults'), so it cannot stand alone before a comma here; 'Much as' must be followed directly by a subject + verb in the same sentence; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase qualifying a preceding element, not a free-standing connector before a new main clause. The conjunct use of 'for all that' is recorded in the Cambridge Grammar of English.

items.text: {1} how carefully we plan, some unforeseen problem always arises on the day.

blanks.hint: ___ + how + adverb + subject + verb = 'it makes no difference how…'

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adverb + subject + verb' ('No matter how carefully we plan') is a concessive of indifference meaning 'it makes no difference how carefully we plan'. The slot is followed by the existing word 'how', and only 'No matter' combines with 'how' in this frame. 'However' already contains the sense of 'how' and so cannot precede a second 'how' ('However how carefully' is ungrammatical — one says 'However carefully we plan'); 'Whatever' pairs with a noun, not with 'how + adverb'; 'Much as' is followed directly by subject + verb, not by 'how'. The 'No matter + wh-word' construction is treated as a universal concessive conditional in the Cambridge Grammar of English.

items.text: Try {1} he might, he could not shift the boulder from the path.

blanks.hint: fixed concessive: Try ___ + subject + might/may = however hard one tries

items.explanation: 'Try as + subject + might/may' is a fixed concessive idiom meaning 'however hard he tried': 'Try as he might' = 'No matter how hard he tried'. 'Much' does not fit this frozen slot ('Try much he might' is not English); 'however' would front the whole clause ('However hard he tried'); 'despite' cannot govern the finite verb 'might'.

items.text: {1} how carefully you plan, some things will always go wrong on the day.

blanks.hint: ___ + how/what/who + clause = it makes no difference that

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adverb + clause' concedes that the outcome is unaffected by any degree: 'No matter how carefully you plan' = 'However carefully you plan'. 'For all' takes a noun phrase, not 'how + clause'; 'Much as' is followed directly by subject + verb; 'Albeit' cannot introduce a finite clause with its own subject.

items.text: Your objections are noted. {1}, the board has decided to proceed with the plan.

blanks.hint: a fixed formal conjunct: 'even if that is true'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed formal conjunct (a subjunctive fossil) meaning 'even if that is true' / 'nevertheless', standing alone before a comma and linking back to the previous sentence. The other options are invented, ungrammatical strings; none is an established English connector.

items.text: Strange {1} it may seem, the two rivals became the closest of friends.

blanks.hint: fronted adjective + ___ + it + may + seem = concessive inversion

items.explanation: 'Adjective + as + it may + verb' ('Strange as it may seem') is a concessive inversion meaning 'although it may seem strange'. 'Though as' doubles the connector and is ungrammatical; 'however' would precede the adjective ('However strange it may seem'); 'that' would make a noun clause, losing the concessive sense.

items.text: The scheme is expensive and slow to set up. {1}, the long-term savings more than justify it.

blanks.hint: a conjunct meaning 'in spite of what was just said'

items.explanation: 'That said' is a sentence conjunct meaning 'in spite of what has just been said', standing alone before a comma and conceding the previous point before countering it. 'Much as' and 'For all' must be followed by a clause or noun phrase within the same sentence; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a full main clause.

items.text: {1} he might, he could not force the rusted lock to turn.

blanks.hint: fronted bare verb + ___ + subject + modal = 'however hard he tried'

items.explanation: 'Try as + subject + might/may/do' is a fixed concessive inversion in which the bare verb is fronted: 'Try as he might' = 'however hard he tried'. 'As try' inverts the words and is ungrammatical; 'However' would need a following adjective/adverb ('However hard'); 'Much as' takes a verb of feeling with its own subject ('Much as he tried'), not the fronted bare verb + subject + modal pattern.

items.text: {1} late it was, the shops on the main square were still crowded with tourists.

blanks.hint: ___ + adjective + subject + verb = 'regardless of how'

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adjective/adverb + subject + verb' means 'regardless of how': 'No matter how late it was'. 'Much as' takes a verb directly, not the adjective 'late'; 'For all' takes a bare noun phrase; 'Albeit' cannot head a finite clause with its own subject ('it was').

items.text: {1} the reasons for the delay, the customers deserve a full refund.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase = 'no matter what' / 'irrespective of'

items.explanation: 'Whatever + noun phrase' has a concessive sense of 'no matter what' / 'irrespective of': 'Whatever the reasons for the delay' = 'no matter what the reasons were'. 'However' would need a following adjective/adverb; 'Much as' takes a subject + verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase qualifying something already stated, not a fronted 'no matter what' phrase.

items.text: The scheme may well save money in the short term. {1}, the long-term costs could be crippling.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct conceding the previous sentence: 'even if that is true'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed subjunctive conjunct meaning 'even if that is true', which concedes the preceding sentence before countering it and stands alone before a comma. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate clause within the same sentence; 'Much as' needs a following subject + verb; 'Notwithstanding' is a preposition requiring a following noun phrase, not a free-standing connector.

items.text: {1} the risks involved, the surgeon judged that operating was the only realistic option.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase = in spite of

items.explanation: 'For all + noun phrase' means 'in spite of': 'For all the risks involved' = 'despite the risks'. 'Even though' and 'While' introduce a finite clause, not a bare noun phrase; 'Much as' must be followed by a subject and verb ('Much as the risks worried her'), not by the bare noun phrase 'the risks involved'.

items.text: {1} how carefully you phrase the criticism, someone on the committee is bound to take offence.

blanks.hint: ___ + how + adverb + finite clause = to whatever degree

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adverb + subject + verb' is a concessive structure meaning 'to whatever degree', so 'No matter how carefully you phrase it' fits before the following 'how'. 'However' cannot precede 'how': it would itself replace it ('However carefully you phrase it'), making 'However how' ungrammatical. 'For all' must be followed by a noun phrase ('For all your care'), not by 'how'. 'Whatever' also takes a noun ('Whatever the wording'), not the adverbial 'how carefully'.

items.text: The scheme is costly and its long-term benefits remain unproven. {1}, the board has resolved to press ahead with it.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct standing alone before a comma + main clause = 'even so'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'even so' or 'granting all that', which stands alone before a comma and a full main clause, linking back to the preceding sentence. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate clause within the sentence, not stand on its own before an independent clause. 'Much though' requires a following verb or adjective ('Much though I admire it'), so it cannot stand alone. 'As it may' is only a fragment of the idiom and is ungrammatical in isolation.

items.text: {1}, he could not persuade the rusty lock to open.

blanks.hint: fronted bare verb + as + subject + modal = however hard he tried

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is the fronted-verb concessive inversion 'Verb + as + subject + might/would', meaning 'however hard he tried'. 'Much as he might' and 'However he might' leave the modal 'might' with no infinitive to complete it and no adverb after 'However', so both are incomplete; 'As much he might' simply scrambles the word order and is ungrammatical.

items.text: The scheme is costly and the timeline dangerously tight. {1}, the board has resolved to proceed.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct linking back: 'even so / that notwithstanding'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed conjunct meaning 'even so', standing alone before a comma and referring back to the previous sentence. 'Much as' requires a following subject + verb; 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate clause within the same sentence, not stand free before a comma; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a full main clause.

items.text: {1} carefully we planned each stage, something always went wrong at the last minute.

blanks.hint: ___ + adverb + subject + verb = to whatever degree

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adverb (carefully) + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of care changed the outcome. 'Whatever' and 'No matter what' govern a noun or clause, not the adverb 'carefully', so 'Whatever carefully' / 'No matter what carefully' are ungrammatical; 'Much as' is followed directly by a subject and verb, not by an adverb.

items.text: {1} as they might, the investigators could not establish how the intruder had entered the building.

blanks.hint: fixed idiom: ___ as + subject + might/would = however hard they tried

items.explanation: 'Try as they might' is a fixed concessive idiom — fronted bare verb + 'as' + subject + 'might/would' — meaning 'however hard they tried'. Since the modal 'might' already follows the subject, the fronted slot must hold the lexical verb 'Try'. 'Hard' and 'Much' are an adverb and a quantifier that would each still need a following verb ('Hard as they might try', 'Much as they tried'), so neither completes 'as they might'; 'Even as they might' is temporal or ungrammatical, not concessive.

items.text: {1} much the authorities invest in flood defences, the low-lying villages remain vulnerable.

blanks.hint: ___ + much + subject + verb = no amount changes the outcome

items.explanation: 'However + much + subject + verb' concedes that no amount of investment alters the result (= 'no matter how much they invest'). 'For all' takes a bare noun phrase ('For all their investment'), not 'much + clause'; 'Much as' would double the quantifier ('Much as much') and is in any case followed directly by a verb, not by 'much'; 'Albeit' cannot head a finite clause with its own subject and verb ('the authorities invest').

items.text: The board's reservations {1}, the chief executive pushed the acquisition through.

blanks.hint: used post-nominally, after its noun phrase: [noun phrase] ___, ... = in spite of

items.explanation: Here 'notwithstanding' is used post-nominally: it follows the noun phrase it governs ('The board's reservations notwithstanding'), a formal alternative to the fronted 'Notwithstanding the board's reservations'. 'Despite' is also a preposition but can only precede its noun, never follow it; 'although' is a subordinating conjunction that must introduce a finite clause, not sit after a bare noun phrase; 'however' requires a following adjective or adverb and an altogether different structure.

items.text: {1} she might, she could not persuade the committee to reconsider its decision.

blanks.hint: fixed idiom: verb + ___ + subject + might/may = however hard one tries

items.explanation: 'Try as she might' is a fixed concessive idiom of the form 'verb + as + subject + might/may', meaning 'however hard she tried'. 'As try' merely reverses the words and is ungrammatical; 'However' must be followed by an adjective or adverb ('However hard she tried'), not stand bare before the subject; 'Much as' takes a verb of feeling or opinion directly after the subject ('Much as I admire her') and cannot supply the fronted lexical verb 'try' that this idiom needs before the modal 'might'.

items.text: The early results are certainly encouraging. {1}, we must not allow ourselves to become complacent.

blanks.hint: fixed subjunctive conjunct standing alone before a comma = 'even so'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed formulaic conjunct using the subjunctive 'be', meaning 'even so' or 'that may be true, but'; it stands alone before a comma and concedes the previous sentence before countering it. 'Even though' is a subordinator that must introduce a finite clause inside the same sentence, not stand alone before a main clause; 'Albeit' introduces a reduced concessive phrase (an adjective or adverbial), not a free-standing sentence connector; 'Whereas' is likewise a subordinating conjunction of contrast that must join two clauses within one sentence and cannot stand alone before a comma.

items.text: {1} the outcome of tomorrow's vote, the government has already forfeited much of its credibility.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase (+ may be) = no matter what

items.explanation: 'Whatever + noun phrase' forms a concessive clause meaning 'no matter what ... may be': 'Whatever the outcome (may be)', with 'may be' commonly omitted to leave a verbless concessive. 'However' must be followed by an adjective or adverb ('However close the vote'), not a bare noun; 'Much as' requires a following subject and verb ('Much as we hoped'); 'Albeit' introduces a subordinate concessive phrase attached to an already-stated point and cannot head an independent 'no matter what' clause.

items.text: The scheme is expensive and logistically complex. {1}, the long-term savings more than justify the initial outlay.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct = 'even so / nevertheless', standing alone before a comma

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'even so / nevertheless'; it stands alone before a comma and is followed by an independent main clause that concedes the previous sentence. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate finite clause, not stand alone before a comma; 'Much as' requires a following subject + verb ('Much as it costs'); 'For all' must be followed by a noun phrase ('For all its expense').

items.text: {1} of his proposal, the timing could hardly have been worse.

blanks.hint: elliptical concession: ___ [may be] + 'of' + noun

items.explanation: 'Whatever the merits (of his proposal)' is an elliptical concessive clause — 'whatever the merits may be' — conceding the point regardless of how the argument is judged, and is uniquely able to be followed by 'of + noun'. 'However' needs a following adjective or adverb, not 'of'; 'Much as' requires a subject + finite verb; 'Even though' must introduce a complete finite clause, not the fragment 'of his proposal'.

items.text: {1} as she might, she could never persuade him to change his mind.

blanks.hint: fixed concessive idiom: ___ + 'as' + subject + 'might/may' = 'however hard she tried'

items.explanation: 'Try as she might' is a fixed concessive inversion of the pattern '[lexical verb] + as + subject + may/might', meaning 'however hard she tried'. 'Much as' must be followed by a verb of feeling or degree ('Much as she admires him') and cannot stand with only the bare modal 'might'; 'However' is not followed by 'as' but by an adjective or adverb ('However hard she tried'); 'Even as' is temporal ('at the very moment that'), not concessive. Only 'Try' completes the idiom.

items.text: The scheme is costly and its benefits far from certain. {1}, the council has resolved to press ahead.

blanks.hint: a free-standing concessive formula meaning 'nevertheless', complete in itself before the comma

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive formula equivalent to 'nevertheless' or 'even so'; it is complete in itself and stands alone before the comma introducing a full main clause. 'Even though' is a subordinator that must be followed immediately by a finite clause, not left standing before an independent one; 'Much as' requires a subject and verb ('Much as it costs'); 'For all' must govern a noun phrase ('For all its cost'). None of the three can occupy a free-standing conjunct slot, so only 'Be that as it may' fits.

items.text: {1} he might, he could never persuade the committee to reconsider its verdict.

blanks.hint: fronts a lexical verb so end-stranded 'might' is licensed: ___ + subject + might/may

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is the fixed concessive pattern 'Try as + subject + might/may', where the fronted verb 'try' is what allows the modal 'might' to stand with no following infinitive (= 'however hard he might try'). Every distractor leaves 'might' stranded because none fronts a lexical verb: 'Much as' must be followed directly by a verb of feeling ('Much as he admires'), not a bare modal; 'However' requires an immediately following gradable adjective/adverb ('However hard he might try'); 'Even if' introduces an ordinary conditional clause and cannot license a verbless 'he might'.

items.text: {1} carefully she explained the rules, the students remained thoroughly confused.

blanks.hint: ___ + adverb + subject + verb = to whatever degree

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adverb + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of an action changes the outcome: 'No matter how carefully she explained'. The distractors all fail before the adverb 'carefully': 'Whatever' governs a noun phrase, not a manner adverb ('Whatever carefully' is ungrammatical); 'Much as' is followed directly by subject + verb with no adverb slot ('Much as she explained'); 'Regardless' is not a conjunction here and would require 'regardless of' plus a noun phrase.

items.text: The prototype exceeded every performance target. {1}, the board still refused to fund full production.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct standing alone before a comma: 'even so / nevertheless'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'even so / nevertheless'; it stands complete before a comma and is followed by an independent main clause. The distractors cannot stand alone in this slot: 'Even though' is a subordinator that must introduce its own finite clause, not sit isolated before a comma and a separate main clause; 'For all' requires a following noun phrase ('For all its success'); 'Much as' requires a following subject + verb ('Much as it impressed us').

items.text: {1} obstacles they encountered on the way, the team pressed on regardless.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun + subject + verb = no matter what/which

items.explanation: 'Whatever + noun + subject + verb' ('Whatever obstacles they encountered') is a concessive nominal relative meaning 'no matter what obstacles they encountered'. 'However' requires a following adjective or adverb ('However many obstacles'), not a bare noun; 'Much as' is followed directly by a subject and verb ('Much as they struggled'); 'Albeit' cannot head a finite clause with its own subject and verb.

items.text: {1} as she might, she could not remember where she had left the documents.

blanks.hint: fronted bare verb + as + subject + might = however hard she tried

items.explanation: 'Verb + as + subject + might/may' ('Try as she might') is an emphatic concessive meaning 'however hard she tried'. The bare verb is fronted before 'as ... might'; only a verb fits this slot. 'However' would need a following adjective/adverb and its own clause; 'Despite' governs a noun or -ing, not 'as she might'; 'Whatever' introduces a noun or nominal clause, not the fixed 'as she might' frame.

items.text: The consultants have raised a number of legitimate concerns. {1}, we have neither the time nor the budget to revisit the design now.

blanks.hint: a fixed concessive conjunct standing alone: 'even if that is so'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'even if that is true', standing alone before a comma and referring back to the previous sentence. 'Much as' must be followed immediately by a subject and a verb of feeling ('Much as we respect them'); 'For all' requires a following noun phrase ('For all their concerns'); 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase such as an adjective or adverbial, never a free-standing connector before a full main clause.

items.text: {1} many times the safety procedure is rehearsed, someone always freezes in a real emergency.

blanks.hint: ___ + quantifier/adverb + clause = to whatever degree

items.explanation: 'No matter how + quantifier/adverb + clause' concedes that to any degree the outcome is unchanged: 'No matter how many times it is rehearsed, someone always freezes.' 'No matter that' takes a bare fact ('No matter that it is rehearsed'), not the fronted quantifier 'many times'; 'Much as' is followed directly by a subject and a verb of feeling, not by 'many times'; 'For all' requires a bare noun phrase ('For all the rehearsals'), not this fronted-quantifier clause.

items.text: The pilot scheme was scrapped after only six months, {1} it had exceeded every target set for it.

blanks.hint: lets a preposition govern a finite clause: ___ + subject + verb

items.explanation: 'Despite the fact that' allows the preposition 'despite' to govern a finite clause: the noun 'the fact' serves as its object and 'that + clause' completes it, giving 'despite the fact that it had exceeded every target'. Bare 'despite' cannot be followed directly by a finite clause ('despite it had exceeded'); 'notwithstanding' likewise takes a noun phrase, not a subject + verb; 'albeit' introduces only a concessive phrase (an adjective or adverbial), not a full clause with its own subject and verb.

items.text: {1} as he might, he could never remember where he had put his keys.

blanks.hint: fixed idiom: ___ as + subject + might/could = 'however hard he tried'

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is a fixed concessive idiom ('___ as + subject + might/could') meaning 'however hard he tried'. 'Much' gives 'Much as he might', which is incomplete because 'much as' needs a verb of feeling ('Much as he loves it'), not a bare modal; 'Hard as he might' is nonstandard — the collocation is 'Hard as he tried', with a lexical verb, not 'might'; 'However' cannot stand before 'as' and would itself open the clause ('However hard he tried').

items.text: The treatment is costly and far from guaranteed. {1}, it offers the only real hope of a cure.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct = 'even so / nevertheless', standing alone before a comma

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'even so / nevertheless'; it stands alone before a comma and links back to the preceding sentence. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate clause within the same sentence, not stand alone; 'Much as' requires a following subject + verb ('Much as it costs'); 'For all' must be followed by a noun phrase ('For all its cost').

items.text: {1} the true reason for his resignation, the timing could hardly have been worse for the party.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase (+ may be) = 'no matter what'

items.explanation: 'Whatever + noun phrase' ('Whatever the true reason') is a universal concessive meaning 'no matter what the reason was'. 'However' must be followed by an adjective or adverb ('However genuine the reason'), not a bare noun phrase; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase qualifying something already stated, not a fronted subject noun phrase; 'For all' would mean 'in spite of', which clashes with a reason presented as unknown ('no matter what it was').

items.text: {1} it should cost me my job, I intend to make these findings public.

blanks.hint: introduces a hypothetical concession (it may or may not happen) + finite clause

items.explanation: 'Even if' introduces a hypothetical, as-yet-unrealised concession: the speaker does not know whether it will cost the job, only that the decision stands regardless. 'Even though' would assert the cost as an established fact, contradicting the hypothetical 'should cost'; 'Much as' needs a verb of feeling and a plain subject + verb ('Much as I value my job'); 'Notwithstanding' is a preposition and must govern a noun phrase ('Notwithstanding the risk'), not a finite clause.

items.text: I have made my decision, and I shall stand by it, {1}.

blanks.hint: a fixed end-placed idiom = 'whatever happens'

items.explanation: 'Come what may' is a fixed universal-concessive idiom meaning 'whatever happens', naturally placed at the end after a comma. 'Even so' is a conjunct that introduces its own main clause ('Even so, I shall stand by it'), not a trailing tag; 'Albeit' must be followed by a concessive phrase (an adjective or prepositional phrase), not left dangling; 'Much as' requires a following subject + verb and cannot close the sentence.

items.text: {1} he might, he could never quite earn his father's unconditional approval.

blanks.hint: fronted verb + ___ + subject + might/may = 'although he tried hard'

items.explanation: 'Try as + subject + might/may' is a fixed literary concessive in which the lexical verb is fronted and followed by 'as' + subject + the modal 'might/may', meaning 'however hard he tried'. 'Much as' must be followed directly by a lexical verb of feeling ('Much as he admires'), not by the bare modal 'might'; 'However' would require a following adjective or adverb ('However hard he might try'); 'For all' governs a noun phrase, not the sequence 'he might'.

items.text: {1}, the board's decision is final and will not be reopened for debate.

blanks.hint: a self-contained concessive conjunct standing alone before a full main clause

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed, self-contained concessive conjunct meaning 'even if that is true', and it stands complete before a comma and a full main clause. 'Much as' cannot stand alone: it demands an immediately following subject + verb ('Much as I sympathise'); 'For all' requires a following noun phrase ('For all its merits'); 'Notwithstanding' as an adverb ('nevertheless') is placed at the end of its clause, not fronted before a comma, and as a preposition it must govern a noun phrase — so none of the distractors can occupy this free-standing slot.

items.text: {1} she might, she could not persuade the committee to reconsider its verdict.

blanks.hint: lexical verb + ___ + subject + might/may = however hard she tries

items.explanation: 'Try as she might' is the fixed concessive pattern 'Verb + as + subject + might/may', meaning 'however hard she tries'; the lexical verb ('try') is fronted before 'as'. 'As much' merely inverts words and leaves no verb of effort; 'However' would require an adverb and its own verb ('However hard she tried'); 'Much as' places a verb of feeling directly after it ('Much as I admire her') and cannot govern the bare modal 'might' with no following lexical verb.

items.text: The scheme is undeniably costly. {1}, the long-term savings more than justify the initial outlay.

blanks.hint: a fixed subjunctive conjunct meaning 'even so / nevertheless'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fossilised subjunctive concessive conjunct meaning 'that may well be true, but even so'; it stands alone before a comma and refers back to the previous sentence. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate finite clause inside a single sentence, not stand free; 'Much as' requires a following subject and verb of feeling; 'For all' must be followed by a noun phrase ('For all its cost'), not used as a free-standing connector.

items.text: {1} the cost, the government is determined to see the flood-defence project through to completion.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase = no matter what the ... may be

items.explanation: 'Whatever + noun phrase' ('Whatever the cost') is an elliptical concessive meaning 'no matter what the cost may be', where 'may be' is understood. 'However' governs an adjective or adverb, not a bare noun ('However high the cost'); 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase qualifying something already stated, not a fronted noun heading a new main clause; 'Much as' requires a following subject and verb.

items.text: {1} they might, the negotiators could not reach a settlement acceptable to both parties.

blanks.hint: fixed inversion: bare verb + as + subject + might/may

items.explanation: 'Try as they might' is a fixed concessive inversion, 'bare verb + as + subject + might/may', meaning 'however hard they tried': the fronted infinitive 'try' is picked up by the modal 'might'. 'As try' reverses the two opening words and is ungrammatical. 'Much as' needs its own lexical verb ('Much as they wanted to' / 'Much as they might wish'); here it would leave the modal 'might' stranded with no verb, so 'Much as they might,' is incomplete. 'However' must be followed by an adjective or adverb ('However hard they tried'), not by a bare subject.

items.text: The scheme is admittedly costly to launch. {1}, the long-term savings more than justify the initial outlay.

blanks.hint: fixed formulaic conjunct = 'even so / nevertheless'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed formulaic conjunct (a fossilised subjunctive) meaning 'even so / nevertheless', standing alone before a comma and conceding the previous statement. 'Be it as that may' scrambles the fixed word order and is ungrammatical. 'Much as that may' is incomplete: 'Much as' demands a following verb of feeling and a full clause ('Much as that may cost us'). 'However may that be' misplaces the modal and, in any case, 'however' would need a following adjective or adverb.

items.text: {1} the northern branch consistently exceeds its targets, the southern office continues to fall short.

blanks.hint: subordinator of contrast: ___ + finite clause set against a second fact

items.explanation: 'Whereas' is a subordinator of contrast meaning 'while, by contrast', introducing a finite clause and setting one fact against another. 'Whereby' means 'by means of which' and needs an antecedent it relates to; 'Whereupon' means 'immediately after which' and signals temporal sequence, not contrast; 'Wherein' means 'in which' and functions as a relative of place or respect. None of the three can head a free-standing clause of contrast.

items.text: {1} tempting the offer may seem, accepting it would compromise our independence.

blanks.hint: ___ + adjective + subject + verb = to whatever degree

items.explanation: 'However + adjective (tempting) + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of the quality changes the outcome ('no matter how tempting the offer seems'). This adjective-fronting slot is exactly where the near-synonyms fail: 'Much as' is followed directly by a verb of feeling ('Much as the offer tempts us'), not by an adjective; 'For all' governs a noun phrase ('For all its appeal'); and 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a finite clause with its own subject and modal ('the offer may seem').

items.text: {1} I sympathise with their predicament, I cannot in good conscience waive the rules for them.

blanks.hint: fronts a verb of feeling: ___ + subject + verb

items.explanation: 'Much as + subject + verb' fronts a verb of feeling for emphasis and means 'although I greatly sympathise'. The distractors differ precisely in what must follow: 'For all' requires a noun phrase ('For all my sympathy'); 'However' requires a following adjective or adverb ('However much I sympathise'), not a bare verb; and 'Despite' is a preposition taking a noun or -ing form, so it cannot govern the finite clause 'I sympathise'.

items.text: {1} he might, he could not persuade the committee to reconsider its decision.

blanks.hint: fixed idiom: ___ + subject + might/may = however hard one tries

items.explanation: 'Try as + subject + might/may' ('Try as he might') is a fixed concessive idiom that fronts the verb, meaning 'however hard he tried'. 'As try' merely inverts the two words and is ungrammatical; 'However' must be followed by an adjective or adverb ('However hard he tried'), not run straight into the subject; 'Much as' requires a lexical verb after the subject ('Much as he tries'), whereas here only the bare modal 'might' follows, leaving no verb for it to govern.

items.text: The costs are undeniably high and the timetable ambitious. {1}, the board has resolved to proceed.

blanks.hint: free-standing conjunct meaning 'even if that is true'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed, free-standing concessive conjunct (a fossilised subjunctive) meaning 'even if that is so / nevertheless'; it stands alone before a comma and a complete main clause. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate clause inside the sentence rather than stand alone; 'Much as' needs a following subject + verb ('Much as it may cost'); 'For all' must be followed by a noun phrase ('For all its cost'), not a bare main clause.

items.text: {1} the obstacles may be, the expedition is determined to reach the summit.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase + 'may be' = of whatever kind or degree

items.explanation: 'Whatever + noun phrase + may be' ('Whatever the obstacles may be') is a concessive meaning 'no matter what the obstacles are'. 'However' must be followed by an adjective or adverb ('However great the obstacles'), not a bare noun phrase; 'No matter' needs 'what' before it can take this clause ('No matter what the obstacles may be'); 'Much as' takes a subject and a lexical verb, whereas 'the obstacles may be' leaves the copula 'be' stranded without a complement.

'As much' inverts the words and cannot precede 'he might'; 'However' requires a following adjective or adverb ('However hard'); 'Even though' introduces an ordinary finite clause and cannot licence the fronted bare verb 'try' that the idiom depends on.

'Even though' is a subordinator that must introduce a finite clause within the same sentence, not stand alone; 'Much as' requires a following subject + verb; 'Notwithstanding', used as a preposition here, would need a following noun phrase, and its adverbial 'nevertheless' sense is not used in this fronted position.
